1693	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
1694	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
1695	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
1696	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
1697	  sequence.
1698	
1699	  \medskip
1700	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
1701	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
1702	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
1703	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
1704	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
1705	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
1706	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
1707	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
1708	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
1709	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
1710	  \[
1711	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
1712	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
1713	  \]
1714	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
1715	
1716	  \medskip
1717	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
1718	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
1719	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
1720	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
1721	  \[
1722	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
1723	    \;=\;
1724	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
1725	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
1726	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
1727	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
1728	    \Bigr),
1729	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
1730	  \]
1731	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
1732	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
1733	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
1734	  higher cohomology.
1735	
1736	  \medskip
1737	  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
1738	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
1739	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
1740	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
1741	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
1742	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
1743	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
1744	  \[
1745	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
1746	    \qquad
1747	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
1748	  \]
1749	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
1750	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
1751	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
1752	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
1753	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}, i.e.\ Mathlib's
1754	  \texttt{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}):
1755	  \[
1756	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
1757	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
1758	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1759	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
1760	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
1761	  \]
1762	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
1763	
1764	  \medskip
1765	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
1766	  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
1767	  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
1768	  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
1769	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
1770	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
1771	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
1772	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
1773	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
1774	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
1775	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
1776	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
1777	  \[
1778	    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
1779	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
1780	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
